_note: MOCK GI patient in the style of synthetic-ambient-fhir-25. No real patient. For PreChart development/eval.

metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for surveillance colonoscopy with severe OSA / anesthesia risk stratification

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: Referral states 'uses CPAP most nights'; no objective compliance download
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient actually wears CPAP only 2-3 nights/week for a few hours, mask leaks and she takes it off; no download in over a year
planted_discrepancies.note: This is the RED-vs-YELLOW anesthesia discriminator: unverified, self-reported 'most nights' is actually poor/undocumented adherence in severe OSA (AHI 42) + BMI 41 -> escalate to anesthesia-directed airway/monitored setting, not routine sedation

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient also takes a 'water pill' (hydrochlorothiazide) every morning
planted_discrepancies.note: unlisted diuretic; relevant to peri-procedural volume status and electrolytes with bowel prep

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient reports a rash/itching from latex gloves

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: severe OSA (AHI 42), HTN, BMI 41; adenoma surveillance indication
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms severe sleep apnea diagnosis, high blood pressure, and prior polyp; here for the follow-up scope

prior_notes.text: Screening colonoscopy to cecum, adequate prep (BBPS 8). Findings: two polyps in the ascending and transverse colon, 6 mm and 9 mm, removed by cold/hot snare polypectomy and retrieved. Pathology (separate report): tubular adenomas, low-grade dysplasia, margins clear. Recommendation: surveillance colonoscopy in 5 years (2026). Sedation note: procedure completed under monitored anesthesia care given elevated BMI and OSA; tolerated well.

Follow-up for severe obstructive sleep apnea (diagnostic polysomnogram AHI 42/hr, nadir SpO2 81%). Auto-CPAP prescribed 6-14 cmH2O with nasal mask. Patient reports intermittent use with mask discomfort and air leak. Counseled on importance of nightly use and offered mask refitting; adherence data not available at this visit. BMI 41. Continue CPAP; return with compliance download. Reinforced weight management referral.

code.text: Obstructive sleep apnea syndrome (disorder) — severe, AHI 42

MedicationRequest._note: C1: patient confirms taking; D2: chart is missing an additional diuretic the patient reports

coding.display: Blood pressure panel with all children optional

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Ascending and transverse colon polyps: tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia. Margins clear.
Severe obstructive sleep apnea, AHI 42/hr, oxygen nadir 81%. Auto-CPAP titration recommended.

transcript:
DR: Afternoon, Ms. Baptiste — thanks for coming in. We're planning your follow-up colonoscopy from the polyps back in 2021. Before we schedule, I want to go over a couple of safety things, especially the sleep apnea. How are you doing?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me grab your vitals, Ms. Baptiste — height's 5 foot 4, weight's 240 pounds. Blood pressure 148 over 86, heart rate 78, temp's 36.8, breathing 16, and your oxygen's 94 percent on room air.
PT: I'm alright. A little nervous about being put under, to be honest.
DR: That's actually exactly what I want to talk through. Your chart says severe sleep apnea — the sleep study showed a pretty high number — and you're on the CPAP machine. The referral notes say you use it most nights. Tell me honestly, how's it really going with the machine?
PT: Well... I try. But the mask leaks and it wakes me up, so a lot of nights I end up pulling it off after an hour or two. If I'm being straight with you, I probably really keep it on maybe two, three nights a week.
DR: I appreciate you being straight — that matters a lot for the anesthesia. Do you have the card or the download from the machine? Sometimes it tracks the hours.
PT: No, nobody's pulled that off in, oh, over a year. Since the sleep doctor last time.
DR: Okay. So we can't confirm the hours, and by your own account it's a few nights a week, not most. That changes how we plan your sedation — I'm going to have anesthesia review you and we'll do the scope somewhere we can watch your airway closely. Bring the machine the day of.
PT: That's fine. Better safe.
DR: Let me check your medicines. I have losartan for blood pressure — still taking that?
PT: Yes, every day. And the little water pill in the morning too.
DR: A water pill — is that a separate tablet from the losartan?
PT: Separate, yeah, a small white one. Hydro-something.
DR: Good to know — that's not on my list, I'll add it. Any allergies?
PT: Not to medicine. But those latex gloves give me a rash, itchy, every time at the dentist.
DR: Thank you — I'll flag latex so the room's set up for that. We'll get you scheduled and send prep instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Baptiste is a 66-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan surveillance colonoscopy (tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia on 2021 colonoscopy, surveillance due 2026). She has severe obstructive sleep apnea (diagnostic PSG AHI 42, nadir SpO2 81%) and morbid obesity (BMI 41). Although the referral records CPAP use "most nights," on direct interview she reports she wears CPAP only approximately 2-3 nights per week for a few hours at a time due to mask leak and discomfort, and no objective compliance download has been obtained in over a year. She reports taking losartan daily and, in addition, a daily morning diuretic ("water pill," hydrochlorothiazide) not present on the medication list. She reports a rash/pruritus with latex glove exposure, not previously documented as an allergy.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 78, RR 16, BP 148/86, SpO2 94% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 109 kg (240 lb), BMI 41.0. Prior diagnostic polysomnogram (2022-08-30): AHI 42/hr (severe OSA), oxygen nadir 81%. No current CPAP adherence data available.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy for prior tubular adenomas — indicated; proceed with scheduling.
- Severe OSA (AHI 42) + morbid obesity (BMI 41) with UNVERIFIED and by-report POOR CPAP adherence (2-3 nights/week, no download >1 year): elevated peri-procedural airway/sedation risk. Per protocol this meets the RED (not YELLOW) tier — refer to anesthesia for pre-procedure evaluation and perform the colonoscopy under anesthesia-directed monitored care in a setting equipped for airway rescue. Patient to bring CPAP machine day-of; request updated compliance download from sleep medicine.
- Update medication list: add hydrochlorothiazide (patient-reported daily use); continue losartan.
- Add allergy/intolerance: latex (contact rash/pruritus) — arrange latex-safe environment for procedure.
- Hypertension — BP mildly elevated today; continue current regimen, recheck.
- Bowel prep instructions and pre-procedure hold instructions to follow; monitor electrolytes given diuretic + prep.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning your follow-up colonoscopy (from the polyps in 2021)
• Your sleep apnea and how often you use the CPAP machine
• Your blood pressure medicines
• Keeping you safe during sedation

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy and the team will send prep and pre-procedure instructions.
• Because your sleep apnea is severe and the CPAP has been hard to use consistently, the anesthesia team will review you first and we will do the procedure where we can watch your breathing closely. Please bring your CPAP machine the day of the procedure.
• If you can, ask your sleep clinic to download the usage data from your machine before the procedure.
• We updated your record: added your daily water pill (hydrochlorothiazide) and noted a latex allergy (skin rash) so the room is set up latex-free.
• Keep taking your losartan and water pill as usual unless we tell you otherwise for the prep.
• Call us with any questions about the prep or your medicines.
